items.question: Define MCAR, MAR, and MNAR in the context of missing data.
items.answer: MCAR: missingness is independent of observed and unobserved values. MAR: missingness depends only on observed variables, not on the missing value itself. MNAR: missingness depends on the unobserved value (e.g. people with high income omit income). Mechanism guides whether deletion, imputation, or indicators are defensible.

items.question: Why must imputation statistics be fit on the training set only?
items.answer: Fill values learned from validation or test rows leak holdout information into preprocessing, producing optimistically biased offline metrics—the same leakage failure as fitting StandardScaler on the full dataset before splitting.

items.question: When would you prefer median imputation over mean imputation?
items.answer: For skewed or heavy-tailed numeric features where outliers pull the mean away from typical values—median is a robust central substitute. Mean is acceptable for roughly symmetric, low-outlier columns or as a quick baseline when mechanism is MCAR and bias impact is small.

items.question: What is a missingness indicator and when is it useful?
items.answer: A binary feature marking whether the original value was imputed or absent. Useful when missingness may be informative (MNAR suspicion) or when optional fields encode structural absence—linear models can learn that "was missing" correlates with the label.

items.question: How do XGBoost and LightGBM handle NaN differently from SimpleImputer?
items.answer: They learn default split directions for missing values during tree construction instead of replacing NaN with a single statistic beforehand. That preserves informative missingness patterns but does not help downstream linear or neural components that still need explicit imputation.

items.question: What are the risks of listwise deletion?
items.answer: It discards all incomplete rows, reducing sample size and statistical power. If missingness is not MCAR, the remaining complete-case sample is biased toward subgroups that fully reported—distorting population estimates and model generalization.
